name: Begonia pavonina
origin: Peninsular Malaysia (endemic — humid shaded lowland to hill forest)
aboutText: Begonia pavonina, described by Ridley, is a rainforest understory species endemic to Peninsular Malaysia — growing on moist forest-floor litter or over rocky humus on gentle slopes and stream banks, in deep shade and very low light where air humidity remains high year-round. It is one of the most visually remarkable plants in the entire Begoniaceae: the upper surface of each asymmetrical, ovate, slightly bullate leaf exhibits a spectacular iridescent blue to peacock-blue structural colouration — not a pigment but an optical effect generated by thin-film interference from the specialised arrangement of chloroplasts in the adaxial epidermis, an adaptation that enhances photon capture in the extreme low light of the forest floor. Deep red undersides frame each leaf. This physics-of-light display gives pavonina its name and its reputation, and makes it the natural choice as the representative species for the AroidAtlas Begonia collection.

quickFacts.light: Very low indirect light (deep shade species)

morphology.leafShape: Asymmetrical, ovate, slightly bullate with scalloped margins
morphology.texture: Slightly bullate, iridescent blue to peacock-blue upper surface, deep red undersides
morphology.variegation: None — iridescence is structural (thin-film interference), not pigment-based

propagation.notes: Most rhizomatous begonias root readily from leaf sections or petiole cuttings placed on moist substrate. Keep warm and humid until plantlets emerge at leaf margins.

careGuide.substrate: Light, well-draining mix: 50% perlite, 30% potting compost, 20% orchid bark. Begonias are susceptible to root rot — excellent drainage is essential.
careGuide.watering: Allow the top half of the substrate to dry between waterings. Water at the base — wet leaves invite fungal problems. Reduce in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70%. More tolerant of average indoor humidity than tropical aroids, but consistent moisture prevents crispy leaf margins.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser at quarter strength every 2–3 weeks during the growing season. Do not feed in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months in spring. Begonias like shallow, wide pots that suit rhizomatous growth. Don't bury the rhizome.

commonProblems.cause: Poor air circulation combined with high humidity
commonProblems.fix: Improve airflow; avoid wetting leaves; treat with a dilute bicarbonate of soda spray

commonProblems.cause: Overwatering, dead leaves left on plant, poor air circulation
commonProblems.fix: Remove dead foliage promptly; increase airflow; reduce watering
Move to a brighter position — begonias need good indirect light to maintain compact growth

items: Check for powdery mildew — a white dusty coating on leaf surfaces, especially in dry conditions; it spreads rapidly between plants
Inspect for mealybugs at leaf axils and the underside of stems where they hide in white cottony deposits
Rhizome begonias should show firm, healthy rhizome tissue — avoid plants with soft, discoloured rhizomes
Most begonias ship well as leaf cuttings — if buying a cutting, ensure it has a healthy petiole and no signs of rot at the cut end

fieldNotes.title: Structural Blue, Peninsular Malaysia
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: Begonia pavonina is extraordinary in a specific, optical sense: the leaf surfaces produce a genuine structural iridescence — an angle-dependent blue-violet shimmer caused by specialised plastid bodies in the upper epidermis that function as thin-film interference structures, manipulating wavelengths of light rather than producing pigment colour. A 2016 paper by researchers at Bristol confirmed the mechanism. Endemic to humid lowland to hill forest in Peninsular Malaysia. In cultivation the iridescence intensifies in lower light conditions, which is somewhat counterintuitive but reflects the forest floor ecology where the adaptation evolved. A genuinely remarkable plant.
